claims.evidence_text: Under reduced doses, wheat responded better to a booster basal dose, followed by at crown-root initiation.
claims.claim_summary: For sewage-irrigated wheat under reduced N doses, a booster basal application followed by crown-root initiation gave the best response.

claims.evidence_text: Thegrain yield of wheat was affectedby the timing of N applicationand was higher when the whole amountof nitrogen was applied basal (Table 2), indicating that a booster dose of nitrogen is essential even under higher accumulationsof organicmatter in the soil.This was fol lowed by the application of nitrogen in two split doses at sowing and crown-root initiation, whereas the lowest yields were obtainedwith delayedapplicationof nitrogen, i.e. in two splits at CRI and milking stage.
claims.claim_summary: Wheat grain yield was highest with full basal N application; lowest with delayed splits at CRI and milking stage (timing of N application).

claims.evidence_text: However, the N-use efficiencywas more (22%) when N was appliedin two splitsasbasal and flowering,whereasNUE was 9-17 per cent.
claims.claim_summary: Highest N-use efficiency (22%) for wheat occurred when N applied in two splits as basal and flowering (timing effect).

claims.evidence_text: The latter also defined the optimal doses of nitrogen, computed to be 55-1 19 kglha.
claims.claim_summary: Optimal nitrogen dose for sewage-irrigated wheat computed at 55-119 kg N/ha depending on soil organic matter.
